seoTitle: How to Share a Udemy Course With Friends the Right Way
metaDescription: Learn what Udemy actually allows when it comes to sharing a course with friends, so you can recommend or collaborate on learning without breaking the rules.
intro: Wanting to share a great Udemy course with a friend is common, but Udemy has specific policies about what sharing actually means on the platform. This covers what's allowed, how to point friends toward a course, and how to collaborate on learning without running into account issues.

steps.heading: Understand Udemy's sharing policies
steps.body: Before sharing anything, get familiar with what Udemy permits, since course access itself is generally tied to individual accounts.

steps.heading: Share the course link or recommendation
steps.body: Pass along the course page so your friend can view the details and enroll under their own account if they're interested.

steps.heading: Consider group or team use cases
steps.body: If you're sharing for a team or business purpose, look into options designed for multiple learners rather than sharing a single login.

steps.heading: Collaborate on the material together
steps.body: Once each of you has access, you can discuss the course content and work through it together even though each account stays separate.

faq.question: Can I just give a friend my Udemy login?
faq.answer: Sharing account credentials isn't how Udemy intends courses to be shared; the recommended way is pointing friends to the course so they enroll separately.

faq.question: Is it allowed to recommend a course to someone?
faq.answer: Yes, recommending or sharing a course link is a normal and permitted way to introduce a course to a friend or colleague.

faq.question: What's the best way to share a course with a work team?
faq.answer: For business or team training use cases, it's worth looking at options designed for multiple learners rather than one individual account.

faq.question: Does sharing a course link cost anything?
faq.answer: Sharing the link itself is free; whether the person who receives it needs to pay depends on the course's own pricing.
